content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
May 10, 1989 
Jane Quan, Administration 
County of Alameda Sheriff's Department 
1225 Fallon Street, Room 103 
Oakland, CA 94612-4381 
Dear Ms. Quan: 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-89-182 
You have been authorized by Sheriff Plummer to request advice 
concerning his reporting requirements under the Political Reform 
Act (the "Act") .17 
QUESTION 
Are travel expenses and per diem received from a nonprofit, 
tax-exempt educational and charitable organization 
considered a gift for purposes of the Act? 
CONCLUSION 
Reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem received from 
a bona fide educational, academic or charitable organization is 
not a gift within the meaning of the Act if the recipient provided 
consideration of equal or greater value to the organization. 
FACTS 
Sheriff Plummer attended a law enforcement mission to Israel 
sponsored and financed by the Anti-Defamation League of B'nai 
B'rith (the "League lf ). 
Sheriff Plummer and nine other top law 
enforcement officers from the united States participated with 
Israeli Police in a program to share ideas on a variety of law 
1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. 
All statutory references 
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Commission 
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 
18000, et seq. 
All references to regulations are to Title 2, 
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 


Jane Quan, Administration 
May 10, 1989 
Page 2 
enforcement matters, including issues relating to counter-
terrorism. 
Sheriff Plummer's travel and per diem were paid for by 
the League, and the value of those costs was approximately $2,500. 
Sheriff Plummer and the others in the group met daily with 
officials of various police agencies, both civilian and military, 
to discuss terrorism, demonstrations and other actions in which 
the police become involved. 
They discussed police problems unique 
to various sights viewed (for example, airports.) 
Sheriff Plummer 
analyzed police functions, and discussed how to handle specific 
situations requiring police response, including rock-throwing. 
He 
gave input as a law enforcement expert with 30 years' experience, 
including his experience with rock-throwing and demonstrations 
involving the People's Park in Berkeley. 
He also gained 
information with respect to the control and monitoring of 
terrorist activities. 
The Anti-Defamation League is qualified as an educational and 
charitable organization by the IRS, qualifying under section 
501(c) (3) of the Internal Revenue Code. 
The League's goal is to 
educate Americans about Israel; promote better interfaith and 
intergroup relations; counteract anti-democratic extremism and 
strengthen democratic values and structures. 
The League maintains 
speakers' bureaus and has several publications relating to law, 
human rights, interreligious concerns and other topics. 
ANALYSIS 
section 82030 (a) defines "income," in part, as any "gift, 
including any gift of food or beverage .... " 
section 82030 (b) 
lists the following exception to "income": 
(2) 
Salary and reimbursement for 
expenses or per diem received from a state, 
local, or federal government agency and 
reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem 
received from a bona fide educational, 
academic, or charitable organization. 
(Emphasis added.) 
section 82028 defines "gift" as follows: 
(a) 
"Gift" means, except as provided in 
subdivision (b), any payment to the extent 
that consideration of equal or greater value 
is not received .... 
Subdivision (b) of section 82028 contains six exceptions to the 
definition of "gift," however an exception for gifts of travel and 
per diem from bona fide charitable, educational or academic 
organizations is not included. 


Jane Quan, Administration 
May 10, 1989 
Page 3 
Based on the language of sections 82028 and 82030, the 
exclusion for travel expenses and per diem received from a bona 
fide educational, academic, or charitable organization set forth 
in Section 82030 is intended to apply to payments made for 
consideration and not to gifts. 
When determining whether or not 
an official has received a gift, the only exclusions which apply 
are those set forth in the exceptions to gift in Section 82028(b). 
(Riddle Advice Letter, No. A-89-200, copy enclosed.) 
Under the above interpretation, the burden would shift to 
Sheriff Plummer to show that he provided consideration of equal or 
greater value. 
If he does not meet this burden of proof, the 
League's payment of his travel expenses and per diem will 
constitute a gift. 
Section 85400 states that an elected of-
ficeholder may not receive a gift in excess of $1,000. 
If Sheriff Plummer has received a gift, he may reimburse the 
League for all or a portion of the gift. 
In that case, the value 
of the gift will be reduced by the amount of reimbursement. 
(Regulation 18726.1(b), copy enclosed.) 
The regulation requires 
that reimbursement occur within 30 days of the date of receipt of 
the gift. 
However, we do not believe that it would be equitable 
to enforce the 30-day rule in this case since your request for 
advice was made during the 30-day period. 
Therefore, if the 
travel was a gift, and Sheriff Plummer reimburses the League 
within 15 days of the date of this letter, we would consider the 
reimbursement to ~e timely. 
Should you have any questions regarding the foregoing, I may 
be reached at (916) 322-5901. 
KED:MWE:aa 
Enclosures 
Sincerely, 
Kathryn E. Donovan 
General Counsel 
;??~$.~ 
By: 
Margaret W. Ellison 
Counsel, Legal Division 


f 
I 
1225 FALLON STREET, ROOM 103, OAKLAND, 
415 272-6866 
CHARLES C, PLUMMER 
March 17, 1989 
SHERIFF 
Margaret Ellison 
California Fair Political Practices Commission 
POBox 807 
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 
Dear Ms. Ellison: 
Since our telephone conversation of March 16, 1989, I have clarified the ques-
tions I had concerning our department stationery. 
We also discussed Sheriff Plummer's attendance at a law enforcement mission to 
Israel this month sponsored and financed by the Anti-Defamation League of 
B'nai B'rith. 
Sheriff Plummer and nine other top law enforcement officers 
from the United States participated with Israeli Police authorities in a pro-
gram to share ideas on a variety of law enforcement matters, including issues 
relating to counter-terrorism. 
The Anti-Defamation League is listed as a charity pursuant to IRS regula-
tions. 
The League's stated goal is to educate Americans about Israel; promote 
better 
interfaith 
and 
intergroup 
relations; 
counteract 
anti-democratic 
extremism and strengthen democratic values and structures. 
The League main-
tains speakers' bureaus and has several publications relating to law, human 
rights, interreligious concerns and other topics. 
The League also sponsors a 
competition for the best books about children written for children and adults. 
Please advise if the seminar qualifies as an educational or academic trip and 
therefore exempt from disclosure. 
Thank you for your assistance. 
Sincerely, 
Jane "Quan 
Administration 
/jq

California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
March 29, 1989 
Jane Quan 
Alameda County Sheriff's Department 
Courthouse 
1225 Fallon Street, Room 103 
Oakland, CA 
94612-4381 
Re: 
Letter No. 89-182 
Dear Ms. Quan: 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act 
was received on March 27, 1989 by the Fair Political Practices 
commission. 
If you have any questions about your advice request, 
you may contact Margaret Ellison an attorney in the Legal 
Division, directly at (916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, or 
more information is needed, you should expect a response within 21 
working days if your request seeks formal written advice. 
If more 
information is needed, the person assigned to prepare a response 
to your request will contact you shortly to advise you as to 
information needed. 
If your request is for informal assistance, 
we will answer it as quickly as we can. 
(See Commission 
Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 18329).) 
You also should be aware that your letter and our response 
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon 
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours,

Diane M. Griffit~/ 
General Counsel

California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
April 7, 1989 
Jane Quan 
Alameda County Sheriff's Department 
1225 Fallon Street, Room 103 
Oakland, CA 94612-4381 
Dear Ms. Quan: 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-89-182 
with respect to your request for advice on behalf of 
Sheriff Plummer, please be advised that we are extending time 
for gift reimbursement pending our determination in this 
matter. 
This letter will acknowledge that you sought advice 
within the 30-day period allowed for return or reimbursement 
of gifts. 
(Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of 
Regulations, Regulation 18726.4.) 
We need to review certain 
facts in connection with your case and anticipate that our 
written advice will not be completed within the 30-day 
reimbursement period. 
We appreciate your continued 
cooperation in this matter. 
Should you have any questions regarding the foregoing, I 
may be reached at (916) 322-5901. 
DMG:MWE:aa 
Sincerely, 
Diane M. Griffiths 
General Counsel 
77l-<--/.!P !£'L 
By: 
Margaret W. Ellison 
Counsel, Legal Division 


California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
June 9, 1989 
Douglas H. Hickling 
Office of county Counsel 
1221 Oak street, Room 463 
Oakland, CA 
94612 
Dear Mr. Hickling: 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-89-l82 
This will confirm that Sheriff Plummer is not required to 
reimburse the Anti-Defamation League for travel and per diem 
relating to the trip to Israel that is in excess of $1,000. 
Because of clarifications by the Commission at its June 6th meet-
ing, we are now advising that the $1,000 limitation contained in 
Government Code section 85400 applies only to payments made for a 
speech, article or published work related to the governmental 
process, excluding actual travel expenses and reasonable 
subsistence directly in connection therewith. 
There is still an issue regarding whether or not the sheriff 
will be required to report the reimbursement for travel and per 
diem as a gift on his statement of economic interest. 
That issue 
will be presented to the Commission at its meeting on July 12, 
1989. 
If you have any questions regarding the above, please contact 
me at (916) 322-5901. 
KED/MWE:aa:HICKLING1 
Sincerely, 
Kathryn E. Donovan 
General Counsel 
By: 
Margaret W. Ellison 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
Are travel expenses and per diem received from a nonprofit, 
tax-exempt educational and charitable organization 
considered a gift for purposes of the Act?
sections.conclusion:
Reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem received from 
a bona fide educational, academic or charitable organization is 
not a gift within the meaning of the Act if the recipient provided 
consideration of equal or greater value to the organization.

Sheriff Plummer attended a law enforcement mission to Israel 
sponsored and financed by the Anti-Defamation League of B'nai 
B'rith (the "League lf ). 
Sheriff Plummer and nine other top law 
enforcement officers from the united States participated with 
Israeli Police in a program to share ideas on a variety of law

Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

Jane Quan, Administration 
May 10, 1989  
enforcement matters, including issues relating to counter-
terrorism. 
Sheriff Plummer's travel and per diem were paid for by 
the League, and the value of those costs was approximately $2,500. 
Sheriff Plummer and the others in the group met daily with 
officials of various police agencies, both civilian and military, 
to discuss terrorism, demonstrations and other actions in which 
the police become involved. 
They discussed police problems unique 
to various sights viewed (for example, airports.) 
Sheriff Plummer 
analyzed police functions, and discussed how to handle specific 
situations requiring police response, including rock-throwing. 
He

sections.analysis:
section 82030 (a) defines "income," in part, as any "gift, 
including any gift of food or beverage .... " 
section 82030 (b) 
lists the following exception to "income": 
(2) 
Salary and reimbursement for 
expenses or per diem received from a state, 
local, or federal government agency and 
reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem 
received from a bona fide educational, 
academic, or charitable organization. 
(Emphasis added.) 
section 82028 defines "gift" as follows: 
(a) 
"Gift" means, except as provided in 
subdivision (b), any payment to the extent 
that consideration of equal or greater value 
is not received .... 
Subdivision (b) of section 82028 contains six exceptions to the 
definition of "gift," however an exception for gifts of travel and 
per diem from bona fide charitable, educational or academic 
organizations is not included. 


Jane Quan, Administration 
May 10, 1989  
Based on the language of sections 82028 and 82030, the 
exclusion for travel expenses and per diem received from a bona 
fide educational, academic, or charitable organization set forth 
in Section 82030 is intended to apply to payments made for 
consideration and not to gifts. 
When determining whether or not 
an official has received a gift, the only exclusions which apply 
are those set forth in the exceptions to gift in Section 82028(b). 
(Riddle Advice Letter, No. A-89-200, copy enclosed.) 
Under the above interpretation, the burden would shift to 
Sheriff Plummer to show that he provided consideration of equal or 
greater value. 
If he does not meet this burden of proof, the 
League's payment of his travel expenses and per diem will 
constitute a gift. 
Section 85400 states that an elected of-
ficeholder may not receive a gift in excess of $1,000. 
If Sheriff Plummer has received a gift, he may reimburse the 
League for all or a portion of the gift. 
In that case, the value 
of the gift will be reduced by the amount of reimbursement. 
(Regulation 18726.1(b), copy enclosed.) 
The regulation requires 
that reimbursement occur within 30 days of the date of receipt of 
the gift. 
However, we do not believe that it would be equitable 
to enforce the 30-day rule in this case since your request for 
advice was made during the 30-day period. 
Therefore, if the 
travel was a gift, and Sheriff Plummer reimburses the League 
within 15 days of the date of this letter, we would consider the 
reimbursement to ~e timely.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Are travel expenses and per diem received from a nonprofit, 
tax-exempt educational and charitable organization 
considered a gift for purposes of the Act?

CONCLUSION: Reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem received from 
a bona fide educational, academic or charitable organization is 
not a gift within the meaning of the Act if the recipient provided 
consideration of equal or greater value to the organization.

Sheriff Plummer's travel and per diem were paid for by the League, and the value of those costs was approximately $2,500. Sheriff Plummer and the others in the group met daily with officials of various police agencies, both civilian and military, to discuss terrorism, demonstrations and other actions in which the police become involved. They discussed police problems unique to various sights viewed (for example, airports.) Sheriff Plummer analyzed police functions, and discussed how to handle specific situations requiring police response, including rock-throwing. He gave input as a law enforcement expert with 30 years' experience, including his experience with rock-throwing and demonstrations involving the People's Park in Berkeley. He also gained information with respect to the control and monitoring of terrorist activities. The Anti-Defamation League is qualified as an educational and charitable organization by the IRS, qualifying under section 501(c) (3) of the Internal Revenue Code. The League's goal is to educate Americans about Israel; promote better interfaith and intergroup relations; counteract anti-democratic extremism and strengthen democratic values and structures. The League maintains speakers' bureaus and has several publications relating to law, human rights, interreligious concerns and other topics.
